---
title: "Predictive Modelling of Coronary Heart Disease in Western Cape Males"
subtitle: "SMM636 Individual Coursework 2025-26"
author: 
  - name: Benjamin Evans
    orcid: 0000-0001-6611-4500
    degrees:
    - PhD
    corresponding: true
    affiliation: City St George's, University of London
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
categories:
  - MSc
  - SMM636
  - 2025-26
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Exploratory data analysis {#sec-exploratory-data-analysis}

- 462 adult males, Western Cape (South Africa), high-risk region for coronary 
heart disease. 
- Nine predictor variables covering blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking 
history, body composition,
family history, psychological profile, alcohol use, and age. 
- outcome is binary: did the patient develop CHD (1) or not (0). No missing values to factor in
 
- @zhu2020adjusting,
the effective severity of class imbalance depends jointly on the ratio and the
feature dimensionality; with only $p = 9$ predictors, this imbalance is
relatively benign and does not necessitate synthetic oversampling.

<!-- ### Paragraph describing class balance and its implications -->
 
- 302 healthy (65.4%) vs 160 CHD-positive (34.6%) — roughly 2:1 ratio
- A naive "always predict no CHD" classifier gets 65% accuracy — any useful
  model must beat this baseline
- Zhu et al. (2020) showed that with only 9 features, this level of imbalance
  is manageable without artificial resampling (SMOTE)
 
<!-- ### Paragraph describing key patterns in the data -->
<!-- **Figure: histograms + correlation matrix side by side.** -->
 
- Age, tobacco, and LDL cholesterol are higher in CHD-positive patients
- Tobacco and alcohol are heavily right-skewed — most patients report low
  usage, but a long tail extends to heavy users
- Adiposity and obesity (BMI) are strongly correlated (r = 0.72) — they
  measure overlapping aspects of body composition
- This redundancy causes problems for modelling: the algorithm can't decide
  how to split credit between two near-identical features
 
<!-- Paragraph describing data preparation -->
 
- Family history (only categorical variable) encoded as binary: Present = 1,
  Absent = 0
- 80/20 stratified train-test split: 369 training, 93 test patients
- Stratified means both sets keep the same 65:35 class ratio
- All features standardised to zero mean and unit variance using training-set
  statistics only — prevents test-set information leaking into the model


# Ridge Logistic Regression {#sec-ridge}

<!-- ### what logistic regression does and why ridge (~100 words) -->
 
- Logistic regression estimates the probability a patient has CHD based on
  a weighted combination of their features
- Each feature gets a coefficient: positive = higher values increase CHD risk,
  negative = decrease it
- Problem: adiposity and obesity are so correlated (r = 0.72) that without
  constraints, the model produces wildly unstable coefficients — it can't
  decide which one matters
- The "ridge" (L2) penalty constrains coefficient sizes, forcing the model to
  share weight across correlated features rather than dumping it all on one
- Contrast with "lasso" (L1) which sets one of the pair to exactly zero —
  discarding potentially useful information
 
<!-- ### how the model was tuned (~80 words) -->
 
- Penalty strength controlled by parameter C (smaller C = stronger penalty)
- To find the best C: training data split into 10 equal folds
- Model trained on 9 folds, tested on the remaining one, rotated through all
  10 combinations (10-fold cross-validation)
- Repeated for 20 candidate C values, logarithmically spaced from 0.0001 to
  10,000
- Best C is the one with the highest average accuracy across all folds

<!-- ### results and interpretation (~100 words) -->
 
- Optimal C = 0.616 → CV accuracy 74.3%, test accuracy 74.2%
- Close match between CV and test = model generalises well, not memorising
  training data
- Standardised coefficients show relative importance of each risk factor:
  age (0.70), family history (0.43), LDL (0.39), Type-A behaviour (0.36),
  tobacco (0.31), blood pressure (0.14)
- Obesity and adiposity near zero — not because they're irrelevant, but
  because the ridge penalty distributes their shared effect evenly
 
<!-- **Figure: coefficient bar chart.** -->
 
- Correctly identifies 80% of healthy patients (precision/recall for class 0)
- Correctly identifies 62% of CHD patients (recall for class 1)
- Lower CHD recall reflects the class imbalance — fewer positive examples to
  learn from
- Useful s a baseline this is probably why we are asked to use it
- establishes a useful lower bound and confirms age, family
  history, cholesterol, and smoking are the dominant linear risk factors
 
# Alternative Classifiers and Model Selection {#sec-alternatives}

<!-- ### Strategy and classifier selection rationale (~100 words) -->
 
- Goal: test whether more complex models can beat the linear ridge baseline
- Three classifiers chosen, each testing a specific hypothesis about the data
  — not selected arbitrarily
- All tuned with the same 10-fold CV framework as ridge, ensuring fair
  comparison
- Hyperparameters optimised via grid search: train across every combination
  of candidate settings, pick the best

<!-- ### Evaluation table -->
 
| Classifier | Why considered | Key params | CV Acc. | Test Acc. | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ridge LR** | Handles correlated features; interpretable | C = 0.616 | 74.3% | 74.2% | **Best** — stable, interpretable |
| **KNN** | Does local patient similarity predict better than global linear trends? | k = 15, uniform | 74.3% | 67.7% | Poor — overlapping profiles degrade distance voting |
| **Random Forest** | Do non-linear interactions (e.g., age × smoking) improve prediction? | depth = 3, 100 trees | 72.9% | 71.0% | Moderate — shallow depth suggests low-complexity signal |
| **SVM (RBF)** | Does projecting into higher dimensions reveal non-linear boundaries? | C = 100, γ = 0.01 | 74.8% | 72.0% | Overfits — best CV but drops on test |
 
<!-- ### what each classifier does, briefly (~100 words) -->
 
- KNN: classifies a new patient by finding the k most similar patients in
  training data, takes a majority vote. If most neighbours had CHD, predict
  CHD
- Random Forest: builds hundreds of decision trees on random subsets of
  data and features, combines predictions by voting
- SVM (RBF): finds the boundary that maximises the gap between healthy
  and CHD patients; the RBF kernel allows this boundary to be curved
- @asimit2022robust examined SVMs from a robustness perspective
- @zhu2017orthogonal showed KNN degrades when clinical profiles overlap
 
<!-- ### interpreting results (~100 words) -->
 
- Ridge LR: highest test accuracy (74.2%), smallest CV-to-test gap 
    -  strong generalisation
- SVM: highest CV accuracy (74.8%) but drops to 72.0% on test 
    -  sign of mild overfitting on a small dataset (n = 462)
- Random Forest: optimal tree depth of just 3 
    - underlying signal is low-complexity, supporting the linear model
- KNN: worst performer 
    -  distance-based methods struggle when patient profiles from both classes occupy overlapping feature space
 
<!-- **Figure: ROC curves.** -->
 
<!-- ### SMOTE and class imbalance (~80 words) -->
 
- SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling) generates artificial CHD-positive
  patients to balance the classes
- Deliberately not applied here
- @zhu2020adjusting: imbalance severity depends on both class ratio AND
  number of features — with only 9 features and 65:35 split, it's manageable
- Minority-class recall of 0.62 confirms the model detects a meaningful
  proportion of CHD cases without resampling

[@zhu2017orthogonal].
@asimit2022robust

# Conclusion

- Ridge logistic regression = best classifier (test accuracy 74.2%)
- Reflects approximately linear separability of risk factors and limited
  sample size
- Primary predictors: age, family history, LDL cholesterol, tobacco —
  consistent with established cardiovascular epidemiology
- Future work: Bayesian uncertainty quantification [@ke2024accelerating] for
  clinically actionable risk intervals instead of point predictions
- Could apply @zhu2018lrid imbalance-dimensionality framework more
  formally on larger cohorts

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}

## Reproducibility

The full analysis code is available as a Jupyter notebook
([`ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.ipynb`](../ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.ipynb)) and as a Python script
<!-- ([`ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.py`](../ml-smm636-a02-heart-disease.py)). -->
The dataset is at [`data/heart-disease.csv`](../data/heart-disease.csv).

:::

# References {.unnumbered}
